content_markdown: The drift velocity of the electrons in a copper wire in which there is an electric current is

options.label: A
options.text: equal to the speed of light.

options.label: B
options.text: close to that of the speed of light.

options.label: C
options.text: of the order of a few kilometres per second.

options.label: D
options.text: of the order of a few millimetres per second.

vibe_explanation: While the electric field (the signal) propagates through a conductor at speeds approaching the speed of light, the actual physical movement of individual electrons (drift velocity $v$) is surprisingly slow due to frequent collisions with the lattice ions. For typical currents in household wiring, this velocity is usually on the order of $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ meters per second, which corresponds to a few millimetres per second.
